title: Robbery as defined in s8 Theft Act 1968
summary: 9084 Criminal — theft plus force or threat of force immediately before or at time of stealing.
learningObjectives: Explain 'uses force on any person' and 'puts in fear'.
Analyse timing — immediately before or at time of stealing.
Apply cases: R v Clouden, R v Dawson and James.

simpleExplanation.title: Theft with force
simpleExplanation.summary: Robbery is completed theft where the thief uses force on a person or puts them in fear of force at the moment of stealing.
steps: All elements of theft must be satisfied.

flashcards.back: A person guilty of theft who, immediately before or at the time of doing so, uses force on any person or puts or seeks to put any person in fear of being then and there subjected to force.
Yes — all s1 theft elements must be satisfied; robbery is an aggravated form of theft.
Force need not be on the victim of the theft — can be on a third party (DPP v D).
Force includes wrenching a handbag from the owner's hand — minimal force on the person suffices.
Pushing the victim so an accomplice could steal the wallet = force for robbery.
Force must be immediately before or at the time of stealing — not after theft is complete.

flashcards.front: What are the two main components of the actus reus of robbery under s8 Theft Act 1968?
flashcards.back: 1. The complete actus reus of theft (appropriation of property belonging to another). 2. The use or threat of force on a person, immediately before or at the time of stealing, and in order to do so.

flashcards.front: What key principle regarding the timing of force in a robbery was established in R v Hale (1979)?
flashcards.back: The principle that theft can be a 'continuing act'. This means force used after the initial appropriation but while the theft is still ongoing (e.g., during an escape) can be considered 'at the time of' the theft.

flashcards.front: How much force is required for a robbery? Cite a relevant case.
flashcards.back: The amount of force is a question of fact for the jury and can be minimal. In R v Dawson and James (1976), jostling a victim to steal their wallet was held to be sufficient force.

flashcards.front: What is the complete mens rea for robbery?
flashcards.back: The mens rea for robbery is the mens rea for theft (dishonesty and an intention to permanently deprive) PLUS the intention to use force or threaten force in order to steal.

flashcards.front: What does 'seeks to put any person in fear' mean in the context of s8?
flashcards.back: It means the defendant intends to make the victim fear immediate force. The victim does not actually have to be afraid; the focus is on the defendant's intention to cause that fear. The threat must be of force 'then and there'.

sections.content: **Robbery s8** (2.2.2) — establish **theft first**, then add **force/fear** element. The force must be **linked in time** to the appropriation.

Robbery is an aggravated form of theft, defined in s8(1) of the Theft Act 1968. The Act states: 'A person is guilty of robbery if he steals, and immediately before or at the time of doing so, and in order to do so, he uses force on any person or puts or seeks to put any person in fear of being then and there subjected to force.' This definition establishes that robbery is not a standalone offence; it is fundamentally a theft that is accompanied by violence or the threat of violence. Consequently, for a conviction of robbery, the prosecution must first prove that a complete theft has occurred, satisfying all the actus reus and mens rea elements under the Theft Act 1968. Without a proven theft, there can be no robbery.

items: Robbery is defined in s8(1) Theft Act 1968.
It is an indictable-only offence with a maximum sentence of life imprisonment.
The offence requires a completed theft (actus reus and mens rea).
The theft must be accompanied by the use or threat of force.

sections.content: In any problem question on robbery, your first step must be to establish whether a theft has been committed. Methodically apply the actus reus (appropriation, property, belonging to another) and mens rea (dishonesty, intention to permanently deprive) of theft before analysing the elements of force.
The Element of 'Force' or 'Threat of Force'

The amount of force required for robbery is a question of fact for the jury and can be surprisingly minimal. The case of R v Dawson and James (1976) established that jostling a victim to steal their wallet was sufficient to be considered 'force'. In contrast, in P v DPP (2012), snatching a cigarette from a victim's hand without touching them was deemed not to involve force. The force must be applied to a person. The alternative to actual force is the threat of force. The defendant must put or 'seek to put' the victim in fear of 'then and there' being subjected to force. This means the threat must be immediate. The victim does not actually need to be frightened, as long as the defendant intended to cause fear.

items: The level of force is a question of fact for the jury.
Minimal force, such as a nudge, can be sufficient (R v Dawson and James).
Snatching property without contact may not be enough force (P v DPP).
The threat of force must be of immediate ('then and there') violence.
The defendant need only 'seek to put' V in fear; V's bravery is no defence.

sections.content: When discussing force, always cite R v Dawson and James to illustrate that the threshold can be low. For threats, explain that the focus is on the defendant's intention to cause fear of immediate harm, not on the victim's reaction.
Timing: 'Immediately Before or at the Time of Stealing'

The timing of the force is a critical element. Section 8 specifies the force must be used 'immediately before or at the time of' the theft. This has been interpreted broadly by the courts. The key case is R v Hale (1979), where the defendants tied up the homeowner after taking her jewellery box. The Court of Appeal held that the appropriation could be a continuing act, and therefore the force was used 'at the time of' the theft. This principle was confirmed in R v Lockley (1995), where a shoplifter used force on a shopkeeper to escape. The court ruled the theft was still in progress during the escape. Therefore, force used to get away can be part of the robbery.

items: Force must be linked in time to the theft.
Theft can be treated as a 'continuing act' (R v Hale).
Force used after the initial appropriation but during the commission of the theft (e.g., to escape) qualifies (R v Lockley).
It is for the jury to decide when the act of stealing is complete.

sections.content: The 'continuing act' doctrine from R v Hale is a powerful tool for the prosecution. In scenarios where force is used during an escape, argue that the theft is not complete until the defendant has successfully got away, thus making the force 'at the time of' the stealing.

The final element of the actus reus is that the force must be used 'in order to' steal. This provides a crucial link between the act of force and the act of theft. If the force is incidental or used for a different purpose, and a theft then occurs as an afterthought, it will not be a robbery. For example, if D gets into a fight with V and punches him, and only then notices V's phone on the floor and decides to take it, this would constitute separate offences of assault and theft, not robbery. The defendant must have the mens rea of intending to use force for the specific purpose of enabling the theft to take place. This ensures that only purposeful violence to steal is captured by this serious offence.

items: The force must be used for the purpose of committing the theft.
Force used for another reason, with the theft being an afterthought, is not robbery.
This element connects the actus reus of force to the mens rea of theft.
The defendant must intend to use force specifically to facilitate the stealing.

sections.content: In a problem question, scrutinise the defendant's motive for using force. If the facts show the force was a calculated part of the plan to take the property, state clearly that this satisfies the 'in order to steal' requirement. If not, argue for two separate, lesser offences.

sections.question: Marc snatches a phone from Tom's hand, causing Tom to stumble. Tom chases Marc; Marc turns and punches Tom before escaping. Analyse whether Marc is guilty of robbery under s8. [10 marks]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — Theft (s1):** Marc **appropriates** the phone (s3), which is **property** (s4) **belonging to another** (s5). If he acted **dishonestly** (Ivey) with **intent to permanently deprive** (s6), theft is made out.

**Step 2 — Force immediately before or at time of stealing (s8):** *R v Clouden* — wrenching/snatching property from the victim's hand is **force on any person**; minimal force suffices. Marc's snatch likely satisfies this **at the moment of appropriation**.

**Step 3 — Punch after the chase:** The punch occurs **after** the theft is complete — force used only to **escape** does not satisfy s8's timing requirement unless it was part of the same continuing appropriation (*R v Hale* — continuing act doctrine may extend the moment of stealing in some snatch cases, but a separate punch after a chase is harder to link).

**Step 4 — Fear of force:** Not required if force is used directly — s8 offers two routes: force **or** putting in fear.

**Conclusion:** Marc is likely **guilty of robbery** based on the **snatch with force** (*Clouden*). The later punch may be a **separate assault** but does not need to be relied on for robbery if the snatch alone involved force at the time of stealing.

faq.q: If someone snatches a handbag from a person's shoulder, is it always robbery?
faq.a: Not necessarily. It depends on the level of force used on the person. If the bag is snatched with enough force to overcome the victim's resistance or cause them to stumble, it is likely robbery, as in R v Clouden (1987). However, if it is snatched without any real force being applied to the person (e.g., it slips off easily), it may just be theft, following the principle in P v DPP (2012). The jury decides if the force was sufficient.

faq.q: What if the force is used on someone other than the owner of the property?
faq.a: It is still robbery. Section 8 states force can be used 'on any person'. For example, if a thief threatens a security guard with a weapon to make them stand aside while the thief steals items from a shop, the thief is using force on the guard to steal property belonging to the shop owner. This satisfies the definition of robbery.

faq.q: Does the defendant have to complete the theft for it to be robbery?
faq.a: Yes, this is a critical point. The definition in s8 begins 'if he steals...'. This means all the elements of theft, both actus reus and mens rea, must be proven. If any element of theft is missing (e.g., the defendant was not dishonest, or did not intend to permanently deprive), then there can be no robbery, even if significant force was used. The defendant might be guilty of a separate offence like assault, but not robbery.

cells: Appropriation of property belonging to another.
Essential element: use or threat of force on a person.
Must be 'immediately before or at the time of' stealing.
Dishonesty and intention to permanently deprive.
Mens rea for theft PLUS intention to use force to steal.
Triable either way (Magistrates' or Crown Court).
